title: Bank competition and household privacy in a digital payment monopoly
abstract: Lenders can exploit households’ payment data to infer their creditworthiness. When households value privacy, they then face a tradeoff between protecting such privacy and attaining better credit conditions. We study how introducing an informationally more intrusive digital payment vehicle affects households’ cash use, credit access, and welfare. A tech monopolist controls the intrusiveness of the new payment method and manipulates information asymmetries among households and oligopolistic banks to extract data contracts that are more lucrative than lending on its own. The laissez-faire equilibrium entails a digital payment vehicle that is more intrusive than socially optimal, providing a rationale for regulation.

author.Itai Agur: IMF, 700 19th St NW, 20431, Washington DC, United States of America
author.Anil Ari: IMF, 700 19th St NW, 20431, Washington DC, United States of America
author.Giovanni Dell’Ariccia: IMF, 700 19th St NW, 20431, Washington DC, United States of America

article.1. Introduction: New payment technologies are making it increasingly easy to collect information about consumer behavior, while network externalities limit the number of viable payment providers and endow them with significant market power in the collection of personal data.1 These data can be parsed to infer a variety of personal characteristics that corporations can profitably exploit, including by deriving credit ratings based on an individual’s spending behavior. In credit markets, such use of payment data can reduce informational asymmetries and unlock market segments where adverse selection may otherwise hinder the provision of credit. There is growing evidence on the expansion of credit access associated with new means of payment, including those issued by major ecommerce or social media platforms known as BigTechs (Beck et al., 2022, Berg et al., 2022, Doerr et al., 2025, Frost et al., 2020) .2 But this may come at the expense of personal privacy. This is an acute concern for many households, as documented in empirical literature discussed in the next section. Further, market power in data collection may give payment providers an edge that alters the competitive landscape in banking markets.How will markets navigate tradeoffs between credit provision and privacy? Who will be the winners and losers of the introduction of new forms of data collection? Does the rise of these new technologies call for government intervention? In this paper, we shed light on these questions by building a model in which a digital payment issuer competes against an anonymous form of payment, cash. This presents households with a choice between a payment method that precludes information collection and another for which the issuer of the digital payment vehicle determines the extent of informational intrusiveness, i.e., what consumer data to collect and retain. For instance, for each transaction the only data collected and retained could be the vendor and the amount spent, similar to what happens with credit cards, or instead reach the level of the specific items bought. Further, the data collector could choose what segments of payment data to connect to other sources of information about a specific individual: e.g., payment data could be linked to users’ activity on social media accounts or ecommerce platforms, as has been the case for, respectively, WeChat Pay and Alipay in China. Alternatively, the payment provider could introduce a digital currency with customizable privacy features.In our model, intrusiveness is a choice variable for the payment provider that determines the probability with which the digital payment vehicle reveals its users’ creditworthiness.Households are consumer-entrepreneurs that differ in their creditworthiness and their care for privacy, both of which are private information. They are endowed with a project but need external financing in order to realize it. The payment provider sells the information it collects about consumer behavior to banks, which can exploit these data to reduce adverse selection when competing for loans to borrowers in an imperfectly competitive market. Spatial differentiation of households and banks provides individual banks with market power, as monitoring costs are lower for nearby borrowers.When optimizing households choose between using cash and the digital payment vehicle, they take into account how being observed by the digital payment issuer affects their personal cost in terms of loss of privacy and the potential revelation of their creditworthiness (and thereby their borrowing conditions). Similarly, the payment provider takes this optimizing behavior into account when determining the intrusiveness of the payment vehicle. How much data the provider manages to collect and the price it can obtain by selling these to lenders depends on the intrusiveness of the vehicle and on the portion of consumers that choose to use it. Lenders observe the result of this game, choose whether or not to buy payment data and can make loan offers conditional on the information obtained.The model’s equilibrium delivers four main results. First, the payment provider makes the payment vehicle as intrusive as possible (leading to certain revelation of creditworthiness type for all users). The payment provider’s optimal strategy stems from adverse selection, which it exploits to instigate a partially unraveling Market for Lemons. This plays on the way that households sort into means of payment. Households with low privacy costs and high creditworthiness want to use the digital payment vehicle, while households with high privacy costs and low creditworthiness opt for cash. In between, there are households that would choose cash if everyone chose cash, but are swept along in a disclosure cascade, because high quality households’ departure from cash worsens the average creditworthiness of the pool of borrowers using cash and hence raises their equilibrium loan rate. As a result of this disclosure externality, the market share of the digital payment vehicle increases with intrusiveness, despite the higher privacy cost experienced by individual users.A second result is that the payment provider maximizes data sale revenues by offering free data access to banks outside their home market. Banks compete à la Bertrand in the credit market and (under symmetric information) a price-limit equilibrium emerges wherein each bank serves and extracts surplus from borrowers in its home market and unsuccessfully contests other markets. Hence, the home bank stands to lose the most (and thus is willing to pay the most) from being cut out of private information about borrowers’ creditworthiness, and its willingness to pay for data is maximized by the threat of an asymmetric equilibrium when out-of-home-market information is free to all banks.The third result is that the payment provider prefers to sell information to the banks rather than becoming a lender and walling off the information for its own use.3 In our model, this outcome stems from the fact that the presence of cash makes the payment provider’s monopoly power partly contestable. Should the provider become a lender itself, it would use the payment information to gain monopoly power over loan markets and thus extract all the surplus from borrowers/consumers. Foreseeing this, consumers would all use cash, denying the digital provider any informational advantage versus competing lenders. Competition among banks here acts as a commitment device for the payment provider to share some of the surplus with borrowers. The ability to credibly commit to lower loan rates would assuage this outcome, but in the absence of perfect commitment devices, this provides a counterbalance to the integration of data collection and credit provision in a data monopoly from, e.g., economies of scope.These results come about in an economy where enough borrowers have positive NPV projects that in equilibrium every project is financed, regardless of borrower revelation status. While there will be winners (households with a low care for privacy and high creditworthiness) and losers (other households), the aggregate effects of introducing the digital payment vehicle are negative: relative to a cash-only world, aggregate output is unchanged and there are positive privacy costs. It follows that under these circumstances a social planner would always prefer a non-informative form of payment.To speak to the potential benefits of an information producing payment vehicle, we next consider an economy where under certain circumstances information disclosure leads to greater aggregate credit access. Put simply, we consider states of the world under which the absence of information about individual borrowers leads to a market shutdown. When the loan market for undifferentiated households freezes, type differentiation generates social value. More intrusive data collection now gives households with good credit quality a way to stand out and obtain credit. This raises total credit provision and aggregate welfare.We use this framework to draw insights about the optimal regulation of a data monopoly, which constitutes our fourth key result. Regulation is envisaged as a slow-moving policy, determined before the economic state materializes, and implemented as a constraint on the intrusiveness of the monopolist’s data collection. Optimal regulation here trades off the costs of intrusiveness when credit provision is assured against its benefits during a credit crunch.Furthermore, we find that there can be a role for a second policy instrument, implemented as a tax or subsidy on households that opt for the monopolist’s payment method. The reason that optimal policy with two tools can raise welfare is that the amount of revealed household data consists of two margins: one extensive (the share of households opting for disclosure) and one intensive (the extent of revelation about such households).We highlight the extent to which the payment data monopoly amplifies externalities in an extension wherein the digital payment system is owned by a consortium of banks rather than a monopolist. The welfare outcome is in between the monopolist and the social planner: the bank consortium neither actively exploits nor leans against the disclosure externalities.Based on extensions, we also find that our results do not hinge on payments being the only source of credit quality data. The key assumption is that the new means of payment reveals more credit quality information, not that it is the sole source of such information. Lastly, we find that our results hold if the ability of the data monopolist to infer credit quality depends positively on the number of users of its payment system.The remainder of the paper is organized as follows. The next section reviews the related literature. Section 3 presents the setup of the model. Section 4 derives the equilibria wherein the data monopolist sells the data to lenders and Section 5 shows why it chooses to do so instead of engaging in credit provision itself. Section 6 analyzes welfare and socially optimal policy design. Section 7 summarizes the model extensions. Proofs and additional modeling detail behind some of the extensions are provided in the Online Appendix.
article.2. Related literature: Our paper builds on classical work on optimal disclosure (Jovanovic, 1982, Grossman and Hart, 1980) and relates to a growing literature on emerging financial technologies in payments and credit provision. Like us, Parlour et al. (2022) and He et al. (2023) consider borrowers that are heterogeneous in both credit quality and a second dimension (respectively, bank affinity and privacy), and data externalities among such borrowers take center stage. In Parlour et al. (2022), payment providers compete against a bank and externalities stem from coupling data garnered from payment services with credit provision. He et al. (2023) also consider lending market competition between banks and financial-technology driven entrants, and show that household data ownership is suboptimal as individual data sharing decisions lead to aggregate credit quality inferences by lenders.4Different from these studies, we consider a data monopolist that stands upstream and controls the flow of information, actively exploiting information asymmetries and data externalities. In essence, we model a BigTech, whereas Parlour et al. (2022) and He et al. (2023) model competing FinTechs (companies that automate financial services, but generally do not enter the market with the dominant networks that BigTechs derive from ecommerce or social media platforms).5 Unlike the FinTechs, our BigTech determines the extent of intrusiveness of its data collection, while it competes with cash, which allows households to opt out of its data web. This lays bare the incentives of a data monopolist to play off households and banks against each other.Several papers consider the role of new forms of digital payments in the preservation of privacy. In Garratt and Van Oordt (2021), the social value of a Central Bank Digital Currency (CBDC) derives from privacy being a public good: taking actions to protect digital privacy is costly to a consumer, while firms use data collected through payments to price discriminate future consumers. In Garratt and Lee (2021), the CBDC raises welfare compared to household ownership of their own data: either policy can fend off the endogenous formation of data monopolies, but household data ownership leads to data underprovision, which hampers firms’ ability to match products to consumer preferences. Ahnert et al. (2022), Kahn and van Oordt (2023), Tan (2024), and Tinn (2024) consider digital payments that are programmable, such as through the inclusion of customizable data sharing features. Different from us, these papers do not focus on hidden quality problems in credit markets.Empirically, although the value of digital privacy in general is debated (Goldfarb and Que, 2023, Chen et al., 2021), for payments and lending specifically most studies find an important role for privacy. Doerr et al. (2023) and Tang (2023) document borrowers’ willingness to pay to limit the intrusiveness of data disclosure in loan applications; and various studies find that privacy is an important determinant of payment choice.6 Moreover, several studies report extensive heterogeneity in privacy preferences among households, in line with our assumption.7
article.3. Model: We consider an economy populated by three sets of agents. Consumer-entrepreneurs, to which we refer to as households, form the first set. Second, there is a monopolist digital payment provider, which, to fix ideas, we term a digital currency issuer .8 Lenders comprise the third set of agents.9 All agents are risk neutral. The economy is distributed across N>3 connected islands. Each island hosts one lender and a continuum of households with mass 1. A lender on the same island as a borrower is called the borrower’s home lender, and borrowers on the same island as a lender are referred to as the lender’s on-island borrowers. All islands share the same physical currency (cash) and digital currency issuer.3.1. HouseholdsEach household is characterized by three variables: its creditworthiness and preference for privacy, which are private information, and the island where it lives, which is publicly observable. The last is self-explanatory. Let us turn to the first two. A household is born with an investment project that can only be brought to fruition with financing from a lender. All projects yield the same payoff, y, when successful and 0 otherwise. However, the probability that a project succeeds, q, differs across households. We refer to q as a household’s credit quality or creditworthiness. Households also differ in their preference for privacy or, put differently, their perceived cost of having their consumption patterns scrutinized. Each household attaches disutility φ to its loss of privacy. On each island, the mass 1 of households is uniformly distributed on a two-dimensional plane with q∈12,1 and φ∈0,2.10 A household’s credit quality and privacy preferences are independent of each other.3.1.1. Household consumptionHouseholds are born with an endowment that they use towards consumption. To avoid complicating the model excessively, we assume that this consumption is needed to meet subsistence needs (i.e., none of the endowment can be saved or used as collateral when requesting credit). However, an individual household’s consumption pattern, if observed, may carry information about its creditworthiness.Each household chooses between cash and the digital currency (DC) to pay for consumption. Cash fully protects the household’s privacy. If instead the household uses the DC then with some probability, θ, the DC issuer is able to infer the household’s creditworthiness.The household experiences privacy disutility φ from being observed by the DC issuer. Households understand the extent to which their behavior is observed by the DC issuer. That extent, captured by θ, determines both the household’s privacy disutility and the probability of type revelation. We assume that households are unable to predict how their consumption choices affect their credit score (i.e., households have no visibility into the algorithm used to extract creditworthiness information from consumption patterns). For a practical example, consider the case of a social media company that can choose whether to link households’ payment and social media data (e.g., WeChat has derived credit ratings from linked payment and social media data). The decision to link these data is observed by households, and disliked (to varying degrees) from a privacy angle by those who choose to use DC. But the way in which social media chats interact with payment data is likely poorly understood by households, and they therefore neither consume nor chat strategically.3.2. Digital currency issuerThe DC issuer is in control of the design of the digital currency. Formally, it chooses the probability, θ, with which it will be able to learn the creditworthiness of individual households using the DC. The DC issuer is a monopolist in the provision of digital payments and hence also sets the price at which its collected digital payment data are made available to lenders. Note that in this section we focus on the case of a DC issuer that puts these data up for sale to lenders and does itself not engage in lending.11 Section 5 considers the possibility that the DC issuer would instead provide credit, either directly or through a subsidiary lender, and shows that this will never emerge as an equilibrium.The DC issuer may charge lenders differentiated fees for information on borrowers on and off their islands. Here, Ωij denotes the data access fee that the digital currency provider charges lender i for access to data on households on island j. We define Dijθ,Ωij as lender i’s demand function for data on households on island j. This demand function will take value 0 (do not purchase) or 1 (purchase) depending on the pricing of the data access, Ωij, and the value that a lender derives from the data.12 How much a lender is willing to spend on data gathered from DC users, will depend on how much borrower data the DC issuer obtained, which in turn relates to the intrusiveness of the DC. Therefore, Dij is written as a function of θ,Ωij. The DC issuer’s objective is to maximize data fee revenue by choosing θ and Ωij. This can be written as (1)maxθ∈0,1,Ωij∑i∈1,N∑j∈1,NΩijDijθ,Ωij3.3. LendersLenders in our model face an infinitely elastic supply of funding at cost cf≥1 and engage in Bertrand competition for loans to households, which (to sustain limit-pricing equilibria) we assume have a breakeven preference in favor of their home lender. Lenders have two sources of differentiation. The first is their access to information about households’ credit quality. If one lender purchases detailed data on a set of households, while other lenders do not, the purchasing lender can charge differentiated loan rates to attract the subset of households with high credit quality, leaving other lenders a pool of lower credit quality borrowers.The second source of differentiation stems from the cost of monitoring borrowers. Unmonitored borrowers are assumed to always run off with the loan principal. Monitoring prevents loan theft with certainty but is costly. Monitoring a borrower on the same island costs a home lender chm. Monitoring at distance is more costly: away (off-island) lenders incur a cost of cam=chm+τ, where τ>0 parameterizes the monitoring cost advantage of home lenders. The relation between physical distance and monitoring costs is well-established in the literature (Agarwal and Hauswald, 2010, Heitz et al., 2023).13 To simplify notation, we let c=cf+chm represent the combined cost of loan provision and monitoring to a home lender and therefore c+τ is that cost for away lenders.We can express lender i’s expected profits in general form (with closed forms following as part of the derivations in Section 4): (2)EqikRikqik−cmih+mia−miaτ+EquRiu−cυih+υia−υiaτ−∑j∈1,NΩijDijθ,Ωij Lender i’s profit consists of three parts. The first is the expected profit on revealed borrowers whose credit type the lender has obtained. Here, mih and mia are endogenous variables representing, respectively, the masses of on-island and off-island revealed households who choose to borrow from lender i. A household, k, whose credit quality data the lender has obtained, and who has chosen to borrow from lender i, is denoted by ik. A lender’s expectation (over borrower types) of the revenue it makes on all loans with customized loan rates Rikqik is EqikRikqik while its cost is c for the mass mih and c+τ for the mass mia. The second term is the expected profit that the lender makes from unrevealed borrowers. The masses of such borrowers on-island and off-island, respectively, are denoted by υih and υia. For every loan that the lender provides to an unrevealed borrower, the expected profit is EquRiu−c and EquRiu−c+τ, respectively, on-island and off-island. Here, iu denotes the unrevealed households that choose to borrow from lender i, Riu is the pooled loan rate that lender i charges unrevealed borrowers, and Equ represents the expected credit quality of households conditional on being unrevealed. The third term is the lender’s cost if it chooses to purchase data on revealed borrowers from the DC issuer, as per (1).3.3.1. Household borrowing and payoffHouseholds are protected by limited liability. They may borrow from any lender. The expected payoff for a household of type q,φ, considering both the potential gains from borrowing and the costs of privacy, can then be written as (3)uq,φ=qmaxy−Ru,0if using cashθqmaxy−Rkq,0+1−θqmaxy−Ru,0−αφθif using DCIf the household chooses cash it expects a return of qmaxy−Ru,0 on its project, where Ru is the prevailing (i.e., lowest-available) loan rate on the unrevealed market; if the household chooses DC, it has a θ probability of becoming revealed, in which case it receives the prevailing loan rate on type-differentiated households, Rkq.14 When a DC user remains unrevealed, which occurs with probability 1−θ, then it borrows at the unrevealed rate, like a cash user. Lastly, the term αφθ represents the privacy cost that a household incurs from using DC, where the parameter α>0 is the common weight on privacy preferences, while φ, as discussed, is the heterogeneous individual privacy preference parameter.For simplicity, the baseline model does not incorporate an additional term to represent an ease-of-transactions benefit of DC relative to cash. We note, however, that cash is merely one example of an alternative payment instrument. For example, our modeled alternative to the DC could equally well be a privacy-preserving crypto asset, which may not have a transaction disadvantage relative to the DC.3.4. Parameter conditionsWe include three parameter conditions. First, we let (4)c<ywhich ensures that the highest quality borrower (q=1) always has a positive NPV project. This is a necessary condition for credit provision to take place. Second, we let (5)τ<y−cbecause competition in lending plays a central role in our analysis and when τ≥y−c, the cost for monitoring at a distance is too large and each lender becomes an uncontested monopoly for its on-island borrowers. Third, we assume that (6)α<c+τ6which is necessary and sufficient to ensure that households with the highest credit quality (q=1) will always want to disclose their type. This helps preserve tractability.3.5. TimingThe timing of agents’ actions and the realization of events is shown in Fig. 1. At Stage 1, households are born with an endowment and a project opportunity, and the DC issuer chooses the DC design, θ, and data access fees, Ωij. At Stage 2, households observe θ and decide whether to transact with DC or cash and consume, after which DC users’ creditworthiness becomes revealed with probability θ. At Stage 3, lenders decide on data access purchases.Download: Download high-res image (261KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 1. Timing and actions.At Stage 4, each lender announces its loan rates. Stage 4 consists of two substages: first, lenders announce a single loan rate towards households whose creditworthiness they are uninformed on, which we call the unrevealed market substage; subsequently, lenders announce their loan rate offers for households that they are informed on (namely, revealed households on which they have purchased data). We term the latter the revealed market substage. This sequencing of loan markets facilitates derivations and is quite standard in the literature (Sharpe, 1990, Petersen and Rajan, 1995, Dell’Ariccia et al., 1999, Dell’Ariccia and Marquez, 2004). For instance, lenders can generally observe market-wide rates (i.e., for generic borrowers, like on our unrevealed market) when they decide on customized offers to (potential) clients that they know more about, such as their own depositors. In our case, the latter group is comprised of households on which lenders have purchased data.Based on the Stage 4 loan rates, in turn, households decide from which lender to borrow to finance their projects at Stage 5. Lastly, at Stage 6, project returns materialize and households with successful projects repay their lenders.
article.4. Equilibria: Below we derive the subgame-perfect equilibrium of the model laid out in Section 3.5. We consider three different cases with varying proportions of good borrowers (with positive NPV projects, i.e., qy>c) and bad borrowers (with negative NPV projects, i.e., qy<c). We obtain this variation by changing the yield of successful projects, as shown in Table 1.Sections 4.1 Equilibrium in the “High, 4.2 Equilibrium in the “Low analyze the “High y” and “Low y” case, respectively. The “Intermediate y” case is a direct extension of the other two cases and is discussed in the Appendix.Table 1. Parameterizing the share of bad borrowers.CaseShare of bad (NPV<0) borrowersValue ofyHighySmally>2c+τIntermediateyMediumy∈43c,2c+τLowyLargey∈c,43c4.1. Equilibrium in the “High y” caseProposition 1When y>2c+τ, all households borrow from their respective home lenders at Stage 5, which, at Stage 4, offer the differentiated loan rates in (7) to revealed borrowers and the single loan rate in (8) to unrevealed borrowers.15 (7)Rkq=c+τq(8)Ru=c+τEqu All lenders obtain data access at Stage 3. At Stage 2, households sort into DC use if (9)φ≤c+ταqEqu−1where (10)Equ=2θ−1+1−θ+43αc+τ24θ−3θ2θ1−43αc+τ2and choose cash otherwise. The mass of revealed borrowers (per island) is given by (11)m=2θc+τ2−43α2−c+τα+c+τ2θ−1+1−θ+43αc+τ24θ−3θc+τ2−43α2 and, at Stage 1, the DC issuer charges each lender positive data access fees for information about its on-island households, and offers each lender free information about off-island households. The DC issuer sets θ=1, which maximizes data fee revenues.ProofSee Appendix A.1. ■In what follows, we describe the main mechanisms at play in the equilibrium, while alluding briefly to the off-equilibrium paths, the detailed discussions of which are left to Appendix A.1. We follow the decision stages of the game in backward order.Stage 5. Households borrow from the lender that offers them the lowest loan rate.Stage 4.When lenders are symmetrically informed (or uninformed), meaning they all bought (or refused to buy) information from the DC issuer at Stage 3, Stage 4 is a standard Bertrand game which admits a unique limit-pricing equilibrium. When all lenders are informed, on each island, competition among symmetric away lenders pushes their expected profits from on-island borrowers to zero, leading them to offer revealed borrowers a rate conditional on their type defined by (7) and unrevealed borrowers the single rate in (8). Each home lender matches these rates and (due to the assumed breakeven rule) serves all borrowers on its island, making positive profits off them due to its positional rent. For the case in which no lender is informed, the same logic applies, with c+τEq emerging as the loan rate that all lenders offer all borrowers.The asymmetric information cases are more intricate and we refer to Appendix A.1 for their details. We note two points arising from these cases. The first is that, if away lenders are informed, the home lender makes more profit when informed than when uninformed. Hindered by its inability to differentiate, the uninformed home lender charges only one loan rate and faces adverse selection among revealed borrowers that receive type-differentiated offers from away lenders. The second point is that when the home lender is informed while the away lenders are not, revealed borrowers do not get lower loan rate offers than unrevealed ones: the informed home lender always matches any undifferentiated rate offer of away lenders for borrowers that the home lender can profitably lend to at that rate.Stage 3.At this stage, lenders decide whether or not to buy the data provided by the DC issuer. At Stage 4, lenders make zero profits on off-island borrowers (due to symmetric Bertrand competition). It follows that, at Stage 3, they are not willing to pay a positive price for information about these borrowers. Hence, they only obtain information on off-island borrowers if they are provided with free data access. In contrast, lenders do make positive profits on their on-island borrowers and they are willing to pay fees up to the difference in expected profits between being informed and uninformed about these borrowers.Stage 2.At Stage 2, households observe the DC design, θ, and the data fees, and foresee the subgame equilibrium prevailing at Stage 4 and hence the expected loan rates and payoffs from using cash versus DC. Based on its q and φ, each household compares its expected payoffs in (3) from using cash (and remaining unrevealed) versus using DC (and becoming revealed to informed lenders with probability θ). When all lenders are informed, then household choice leads to the sorting condition in (9). Note that since expected loan rates for unrevealed borrowers depend on the credit quality of the pool of unrevealed households, a household’s payment choice is affected by other households’ payment choices.A comparison to a cash-only (or, equivalently, θ=0) setting is useful to highlight these interactions. Without DC, all households use cash so that Equ=Eq=34 and Ru=43c+τ. At this loan rate, some households will find it attractive to use a θ>0 DC, when given that option. This set of households is to the right of and below the dashed line in Fig. 2. Since these households are located in the corner with the highest credit quality and some of them become revealed, their choice to use DC lowers Equ and raises Ru. With a lower Equ, more households find that the condition in (9) holds, and choose DC over cash. These households are near (to the left of) the threshold given by the dashed line, meaning that their credit quality is high among cash users. Their choice to use the DC in turn further lowers Equ, and so on. The equilibrium in Proposition 1 documents the ultimate settle point of such a process, as represented by the unbroken line in Fig. 2. But behind this equilibrium lies a cascade of disclosure: households that only choose to use the DC because other, higher credit quality households choose to do so. Households in between the dashed and unbroken lines in Fig. 2 can be worse off than if the DC did not exist, which highlights the public good nature of privacy here.The subgame where all lenders are informed has the highest DC use among households, because every revealed household is offered a type-differentiated rate. When all lenders are uninformed, type differentiation is obviously impossible and therefore all households would choose cash at Stage 2 if they expected that subgame for Stage 4, since cash comes without a privacy cost. When the home lender is informed but the away lenders are not then, as noted at Stage 4, revealed borrowers do not obtain lower loan rates than the unrevealed and, therefore, all households again choose cash at Stage 2. When the home lender is uninformed and competes against informed away lenders, those households that foresee that they can obtain a lower undifferentiated loan rate from the home lender than a differentiated rate from the away lenders, always choose cash. This leads to (weakly) more cash use than when all lenders are informed.Download: Download high-res image (164KB)Download: Download full-size imageFig. 2. Payment choice and disclosure cascades.Stage 1. The DC issuer sets θ and data access fees to maximize its profit. Based on Stage 2, it foresees zero DC uptake (and zero profit) when the away lenders are uninformed. Away lenders cannot pay a positive fee for data access and therefore the DC issuer gives them the data for free. It then charges the home lender a data access fee equal to the profit that the lender gains from being informed, which we show (in Appendix A.1) is maximized for θ=1. Hence, the DC issuer designs a maximally intrusive DC.164.2. Equilibrium in the “Low y” caseProposition 2When y∈c,43c: households that can borrow do so from their respective home lenders at Stage 5. At Stage 4, revealed borrower loan rates are given by (7), while lenders make no loan rate offers to the unrevealed. At Stage 3, lenders obtain data access from the DC issuer. At Stage 2, households with (12)q≥αφ+c+τychoose to use the DC and others use cash. At Stage 1, the DC issuer sets θ=1, while offering lenders free data on their off-island borrowers and charging them the fee mτ for data on their on-island borrowers where (13)m=θ2αyy−c−τ2ify<2α+c+τ2θ1−α+c+τyify>2α+c+τProofSee Appendix A.2. A summary of the main mechanisms is included below. ■Stage 5. Each household borrows from the lender that offers it the lowest loan rate.Stage 4. When y∈c,43c, uninformed lenders never make loan offers. The intuition for this is straightforward: y<43c means that lending to all households is never viable, because Eqy−c=34y−c<0, where y is the highest conceivable loan rate and c is the lowest cost of providing loans. An uninformed lender can never have a better expected credit quality than Eq on its pool of borrowers: if some other lender(s) is (are) informed, then the uninformed lender faces adverse selection, leaving it with an average borrower quality below Eq; if, instead, all lenders are uninformed, then all lenders face a borrower pool of quality Eq.Thus, only informed lenders make loan offers. When all lenders are informed, this leads to the usual limit pricing equilibrium towards those borrowers that are viable for all lenders. We note that with y∈c,43c, there are some households that only have positive NPV projects when borrowing from the home lender, and the home lender is an uncontested monopolist (charging y) towards such households, if they are revealed. When only the home lender is informed, then it is an uncontested monopolist towards all viable households. And when the home lender is uninformed and the away lenders are informed, limit pricing emerges (towards viable households).Stage 3. Lenders make no profits when uninformed and therefore are willing to pay up to all the profits they make when informed to obtain data access.Stage 2. Whenever lenders are uninformed about off-island borrowers, all households choose cash because: (a) when their home lender also does not have access to information about them, no credit provision takes place and there is no benefit to choosing DC; (b) when instead their home lender is informed, then at Stage 4 it is an uncontested monopolist and extracts all project value from revealed households and, foreseeing this, all households choose cash at Stage 2, from the angle of privacy preservation.When lenders are informed about off-island borrowers, we can distinguish three groups of households: (1) households with q<cy choose cash, because their projects are unviable to all lenders; (2) households with q∈cy,c+τy also choose cash because they either face an uncontested monopolist lender (if their home lender is informed) or are credit excluded (when their home lender is uninformed); (3) households with q>c+τy face a limit pricing environment because their projects are viable to away lenders. Among such households, those that satisfy the condition in (12) choose DC.Stage 1. The DC issuer provides lenders with free data access on off-island households, since otherwise all households choose cash at Stage 2. The DC issuer charges lenders a fee (marginally below) the full profits that they make on their on-island revealed households, mτ, which it knows the lenders will accept at Stage 3. The DC issuer then optimizes profits by maximizing m and this is achieved by setting θ=1 (as apparent from (13)).
article.5. Data usage strategies: So far, we have focused on a DC issuer that markets its data to lenders. This section considers a DC issuer that uses its data advantage to engage in lending on its own, without selling data to other lenders. The setup has an identical structure of lenders as in the baseline, with one lender based on each island. We assume that, in addition, the DC issuer has a branch on every island. Thus, if the DC issuer provides credit, its cost of monitoring borrowers is the same as a home lender. The timing of the game is as described in Section 3.5, except that: at Stage 1, the DC issuer only determines θ but does not set data access fees; Stage 3 on lender data purchases is inactive (since no data are put up for sale); and at Stage 4, both the DC issuer and all lenders set loan rates.This setup leads to a stark outcome, formalized in Proposition 3: all households choose cash and the DC issuer makes no profit. The reason is that, regardless of its q, a revealed borrower never obtains a lower borrowing cost than unrevealed borrowers. In the “High y” case, all borrowers are offered loan rate cEqu in the Stage 4 equilibrium; and in the “Low y” case, uninformed lenders do not bid at all due to adverse selection, like in Proposition 2, meaning that, towards borrowers to which it can profitably lend, the informed DC issuer is an uncontested monopoly and charges rate y.Proposition 3The DC issuer makes zero profit if it provides loans rather than selling data access to lenders. This holds for both the “High y” and “Low y ” cases in Table 1.ProofSee Appendix A.3. ■The fundamental problem for the DC issuer here is the ability of households to opt out of DC use when they foresee undifferentiated loan rates, which originate in the loan market power of a sole informed lender. Unmodeled factors, like economies of scope, may plead for the integration of data collection and credit provision. Nevertheless, the notion that there is a benefit for the data monopolist to be a “puppet master”, who manipulates information asymmetries from above but does not directly get involved in credit markets, is powerful; and so is the absence of a fully credible way to pre-commit not to exploit the market power inherent in a data monopoly, after households have given up their data (Liu et al., 2025) . Market power in lending is a double-edged sword in a setting like this: it could facilitate higher loan revenues, but only if enough households stay put and the prospect of rent extraction does not push too many of them to opt out of disclosure altogether.17
article.6. Policy analysis: We now draw insights from our model for the analysis and design of socially optimal policy. In particular, we consider the role of regulation to constrain the DC’s intrusiveness.To enable the welfare analysis, we extend our model by adding two more stages to the game described in Section 3.5. First, we assume that the policy maker determines regulation at the beginning of the game, which represents the notion that regulation is a long-term policy that is infrequently revised. We denote socially optimal DC design by θ¯ and implement regulation as a constraint on the intrusiveness of data collection.18 That is, the DC issuer now faces a constrained design choice such that θ∈0,θ¯. Second, we let y represent an uncertain aggregate state, exogenously determined in a third stage: both DC policy and DC design are set before y is known (and the remaining stages, namely 2–6 in Section 3.5, now become Stages 4-8). With probability γ∈0,1, y takes the value yh and the good economic state of Proposition 1 materializes. With probability 1−γ, a bad state, yl, occurs, in which credit risks are profound and there are relatively many bad borrowers, as in Proposition 2. That is, we let yh represent “High y ” in Table 1, with yh>2c+τ, while yl represents “Low y” in Table 1 with yl∈c,43c.6.1. Welfare expressionWe define aggregate social welfare as the sum of the expected payoffs of all households and the total profits of lenders and the DC issuer. Welfare is then determined by two factors only. The first is the value added of the projects that receive funding. From Proposition 1, Proposition 2, in equilibrium households borrow from home lenders, so that a project’s expected NPV is qy−c. Some of this value added accrues to households, through positive net returns on projects, and some of the value added is captured as profits by the digital payment issuer and lenders. The second factor is the privacy costs borne by DC users. In general form, welfare can be expressed as: (14)W=∫borrowersqy−cfq“borrowing”dq−∫DC usersαφθgφ“DC use”dφAt the policy maker’s decision stage, the state of the economy is uncertain, and therefore the policy maker’s objective is to maximize expected welfare, EW, with respect to θ:Proposition 4The policy maker’s objective is given by (15)maxθEW=maxθγWh+1−γWl(16)Wh=34yh−c−2αθ1−1+4α3c+τEqu(17)Wl=θyl−c+τ2yl−c+2τ6αylθyl−cyl−c−2α+43α2−τ2ylifyl<2α+c+τifyl>2α+c+τ where Wh and Wl represent welfare in the good and bad states, respectively, and the closed-form for Equ is in (10).ProofSee Appendix A.4. ■6.2. Socially optimal regulationIn the bad state, welfare increases linearly with θ, as seen from (17). A higher θ helps households who want to reveal their types to do so, while there are no disclosure externalities when the loan market for unrevealed households is inoperative. Instead, in the good state, all households receive loans and therefore the total value added of projects is independent of θ. The DC’s impact on aggregate welfare then runs through privacy costs. A higher θ has a direct effect on privacy costs in (3) and in addition has an indirect effect from disclosure cascades. These effects are seen from, respectively, the terms θ and Equ in (16). Put together, direct and indirect effects imply that Wh decreases more than linearly with θ. This nonlinearity creates the potential for an interior solution for socially optimal policy, derived in Proposition 5.Proposition 5When the good state is certain (γ=1), privacy costs dominate and the policy maker bans the DC, θ¯=0. When the bad state is certain (γ=0), credit inclusion from type differentiation dominates and the laissez-faire policy, θ¯=1, is socially optimal. When γ∈0,1, the policy maker for some parameterizations chooses to regulate the DC with θ¯∈0,1 given by (18)θ¯=332α24α2+c+τ2−c+τ2−12α28α2γα+c+31−γλc+τ2+4α22γτ−1−γλ216α4γ2+48α31−γγλ+91−γ2λ2c+τ2+12α21−γλ4γc+τ−1−γλwhere (19)λ≡yl−c+τ2yl−c+2τ6αylyl−cyl−c−2α+43α2−τ2ylifyl<2α+c+τifyl>2α+c+τProofSee Appendix A.5. ■6.3. Distributional effectsThe distributional effects from DC design differ between the two states of y. In the bad state, DC users’ disclosure is a Pareto improvement, creating value to them, no externalities to others, and profits to the DC issuer. In the good state, some (high q, low φ) households gain from a higher θ through lower customized loan rates. But their gains are exactly offset by the losses of unrevealed households, who face a higher loan rate.19 Moreover, although with a higher θ the DC issuer gains fee revenues at lenders’ expense, the sum of DC issuer and lender profits is unchanged. The impact of θ on Wh therefore derives from the one effect that has no offset: the privacy loss of households that are swept along in disclosure cascades.
article.7. Model extensions: This section considers four extensions to the baseline model. The mathematical derivations for two of these extensions are contained in the Online Appendix.207.1. A second policy instrumentHousehold data disclosure depends on two margins in our model: who sorts into DC use, which we refer to as the extensive margin of disclosure; and how much information is disclosed about DC-using households, which we term the intensive margin. By constraining the intensive margin, regulation also affects sorting into means of payment and thereby the extensive margin. But the fact that there are two margins implies room for a second instrument to improve welfare outcomes.We explore such an instrument in the form of a government tax or subsidy on DC use (e.g., on DC transactions or on opening a user wallet), denoted by T, where T>0 represents a tax and T<0 is a subsidy.21 We rederive the model with the inclusion of T in Appendix B.1 and find that for some parameterizations the availability of a second instrument does not raise welfare (e.g., when banning DC is optimal), while for other parameterizations it does. The most surprising outcome is that subsidizing DC use is sometimes socially optimal. A subsidy can optimally lean against underdisclosure, which occurs in the bad state because lender market power establishes loan rates above c and some positive NPV households choose cash.7.2. Alternative sources of credit qualityIn the baseline model, the DC is the sole source of credit quality information. While adding lender-specific household data lies outside the scope of the current analysis, we can incorporate alternative public sources of information. Appendix B.2 considers two possibilities. The first is pre-existing public information on borrowers, such as from credit registries or FICO scores. The second is public payment system data, such as from a data-sharing CBDC, where we assume that the privately-issued DC is more informationally intrusive than the public alternative. Appendix B.2 finds that both possibilities lead back to our baseline results, because sorting conditions between means of payment are unchanged. The key factor for our results is that the DC is more informationally intrusive than other payment alternatives, not that it is the sole source of credit quality data.7.3. Lender consortiumThis extension lets a consortium of lenders (as in, e.g., Allen and Gale (1999) and Liberti et al. (2022)) own the DC and choose θ. We assume that the collected DC data are now freely available to all lenders. While lenders cooperate in DC design, their mode of competition on the credit market is the same as in the baseline. In the good state, a lender’s total equilibrium profit is τ.22 Therefore, lenders’ profits do not depend on θ and the lender consortium is indifferent about θ in this case: privately optimal θ is now indeterminate. Unlike the DC issuer, the lender consortium does not actively exploit disclosure externalities; unlike a social planner, the consortium does not lean against such externalities either. Instead, in the bad state, only revealed households receive credit and the mass of such households rises as θ increases, as seen from (13), so that the consortium sets θ=1. In the bad state, θ=1 creates value for households and lenders, and is optimal for the DC issuer, lender consortium and social planner alike.7.4. User base and inference qualityThe DC issuer’s inference quality could depend on the size of its user base, as screening algorithms can become more effective when trained on a larger group of borrowers (Ghosh et al., 2025). We here write the probability of type revelation as a function pθ,μθ where μθ is the mass of DC users and p rises both as θ increases and as μθ increases. Due to disclosure cascades, μ′θ>0 (Section 4.1) and therefore dpθ,μθdθ=∂pθ,μθ∂θ+∂pθ,μθ∂μθμ′θ>0. If we normalize p1,μ1=1 (i.e., with the largest attainable mass of DC users, which occurs at θ=1, the inference technology works perfectly), then equilibrium expressions are identical to our baseline. With θ=1, the DC issuer now plays on both households’ disclosure externality and the network effect that improves its inference technology.
article.8. Conclusion: New technologies are fundamentally changing the way that payments and lending take place, and create an unprecedented scope for data monopolization. Such developments can bring both unique opportunities and risks to consumers and borrowers, and policy institutions are actively grappling with the extent to which regulation should step in. This paper analyzes how private and social optima diverge when a monopolist offers privacy-valuing households a digital payment system that collects data on their habits and can help them signal their creditworthiness to lenders. We find that the monopolist uses its position to play off both households and lenders against each other. To maximize the value of its household data, the monopolist makes its data collection as intrusive as possible. This squeezes households into a partially unraveling Market for Lemons, whereby the willingness of some high quality households to disclose prods more tranches of households to use the digital payment instrument.Socially optimal data regulation aims at shielding households from being played off against each other. The challenge in doing so, is that regulation is a long-term policy, while the benefits from counteracting intrusive data collection are state dependent. In a bad state, where projects become less profitable, credit provision to unrevealed borrowers can freeze. Data gathering can then be positive for aggregate welfare because the data unfreeze part of the credit market by differentiating better quality households. Socially optimal regulation trades off this improved credit supply during downturns against the privacy losses of households from disclosure cascades in upturns.Future work can build on the framework provided here to consider competition between multiple payment providers, which could include a public provider, such as a CBDC. Notably, our framework can already be interpreted from the angle of a benchmark case wherein CBDC is introduced with data-sharing features as the only alternative to cash. What we discussed as optimal regulation (θ¯) of a private digital payment system, can equivalently be considered the optimal degree of data collection with a welfare-maximizing CBDC.Future work can also extend this framework to encompass elements of household data ownership. Policies that allow households to transfer their payment data across lenders have been implemented in various countries, and the implications of such open banking policies for credit market outcomes are a subject of active study (Babina et al., 2025, Brunnermeier and Payne, 2024, He et al., 2023, Nam, 2022, Alok et al., 2024). Moreover, the EU and California have recently implemented regulation that endows households with a degree of digital data ownership, including the ability to opt out of the sharing of their data. Doerr et al. (2023) study how California’s Consumer Privacy Act has affected credit market outcomes, particularly for FinTechs that rely on the purchase of such data.In our model, the monopolist’s ability to profitably sell data about households’ creditworthiness partly stems from the adverse selection suffered by a lender that would choose not to purchase this information. In a setting that would incorporate costs to collecting and processing payment data, policies that allow households to credibly transmit their payment data to lenders would reduce the incentives to collect their data in the first place. Data ownership policies could then complement or replace intrusive regulation directed at the design of the payment technology. We leave a formal analysis of this issue to future research.
